title: An RNA-guided ‘chromatin shredder’ for selective cell killing - Nature Biotechnology
text:
Access through your institution

Buy or subscribe

Many common cancer-driving proteins remain undruggable because they lack defined drug-binding pockets. Writing in Nature, Zeng et al. repurpose CRISPR–Cas12a2, an RNA-guided nuclease with trans-nucleolytic cleavage activities, to selectively eliminate cancer cells in response to specific mutant transcripts.Beyond degradation of naked nucleic acids in trans, Cas12a2 also cleaves chromatin in mammalian cells following RNA target recognition. To achieve selective cancer cell killing, the authors use purified SuCas12a2 ribonucleoproteins and guide RNAs (gRNAs) that target mRNAs containing compatible protospacer flanking sites — sequences required for SuCas12a2 activation. This system enables discrimination between transcripts differing by a single nucleotide, allowing selective targeting of hotspot TP53 mutations (R248Q, R280K and E285K) for which no approved therapies currently exist. The system is also shown to eliminate cancer cells with oncogene overexpression and to target cancer-specific neo-junctions generated by EGFR in-frame deletions. Notably, in vivo delivery of Cas12a2 mRNA and its gRNA targeting MYC or p53 R248Q transcripts using lipid nanoparticles reduces tumor burden in mouse models of MYC-driven liver cancer and TP53-mutant lung cancer.
